items.passage: Keystone species are organisms that exert an unusually strong influence on the structure of their ecosystems. When such a species disappears, the entire community can change in dramatic ways. Sea otters, for example, keep sea urchin populations low and protect valuable kelp forests. Without these predators, the urchins multiply quickly and strip the seabed of plant life. Scientists therefore study keystone species carefully to understand how healthy habitats remain balanced over time.

Light pollution is the excessive artificial brightness that spreads across the night sky above modern cities. This glow comes from streetlights, buildings, and signs that shine upward into the air. As a result, faint stars become difficult to see even on a clear evening. Many nocturnal animals are confused by the artificial light and lose their natural rhythms. However, shielded lamps and dimmer bulbs can reduce this waste and restore darker skies.

The anchoring effect is a mental shortcut in which the first number strongly shapes later judgments. When shoppers see a high original price, a discounted figure suddenly seems like a bargain. The initial value acts as an anchor that pulls every following estimate toward itself. Even experts are influenced by these early figures, though they rarely notice the effect. Understanding this bias helps consumers pause and question the numbers presented to them.

The transatlantic telegraph cable was an ambitious engineering project that finally connected two continents beneath the ocean. Before its completion, messages between Europe and America traveled slowly by ship across the sea. The first working cable was laid in 1858 after several expensive and discouraging failures. Signals that once needed ten days could now cross the Atlantic within a few minutes. This invention transformed communication and marked the beginning of a truly connected global network.

Limestone caves are underground chambers that form slowly as acidic water dissolves soft rock over centuries. Rainwater absorbs carbon dioxide from the surrounding air and becomes a weak natural acid. This acid slowly seeps through tiny cracks and gradually widens them into hollow passages. Inside these caves, mineral deposits build spectacular columns that hang from the ceiling. Because the process is extremely slow, a single formation may require thousands of years.

Supermassive black holes are enormous objects that sit quietly at the center of most large galaxies. Their gravity is so powerful that not even light can escape from their surface. Astronomers cannot observe them directly, so instead they study the bright gas swirling nearby. As surrounding matter falls inward, it heats up and releases intense bursts of radiation. These hidden giants shape entire galaxies and influence how new generations of stars form.
